content.pattern.capslock_variant.descrip: When CapsLock is on, each affected key produces a different character than it does when CapsLock is off — not the uppercase of the same letter, but a distinct character from elsewhere in the script. This is used for scripts that have no upper/lower case and need more character slots than Shift alone provides, so CapsLock becomes a stable "alternate letter" layer. Use sparingly: CapsLock gives users no reliable on-screen cue that the mode is active, so anyone unfamiliar with the design can be surprised by the output.
content.pattern.capslock_variant.questio: The normal (CapsLock-OFF) output characters, in the same order as the keys above — one character per key, typed without spaces. This list must be the same length as the caps-on output list.
The CapsLock-ON output characters, in the same order as the keys above — one character per key, typed without spaces. Must be the same length as the caps-off output list.

Which keys produce a different character under CapsLock, in the CapsLock-OFF (normal) state? Write each key with the NCAPS modifier so it matches only when CapsLock is off, back-to-back with no separators, e.g. [NCAPS K_C][NCAPS K_H]. The order here must match the caps-off output list below one-for-one.
The same keys as above, but written with the CAPS modifier so they match when CapsLock is on, e.g. [CAPS K_C][CAPS K_H]. Keep the same order as the caps-off key list.
content.pattern.capslock_variant.title: CapsLock switches to a different letter
content.pattern.constraints_beep.descrip: Add input validation to any primary strategy. Invalid base-diacritic or character-cluster combinations trigger a beep and suppression. The user receives immediate audio feedback that they typed something the keyboard doesn't support. Works with S-02 (deadkeys), S-05 (transliteration), S-07 (cycling), S-09 (clusters) — any strategy where some combinations are valid and others are not.
content.pattern.constraints_beep.questio:
List any explicitly INVALID combinations (optional). Format: base,diacritic → reject
Example for el_pasifika:
  consonant + acute → INVALID (accents only on vowels)
  vowel + macron → valid for some vowels, invalid for others

If not specified, any base + diacritic that isn't in the main rule set is considered invalid and will trigger the beep.

Which base characters (consonants, vowels, syllables) can accept diacritics? Format: one per line, comma-separated: base_character_or_keystroke,unicode_codepoint
Example for Polynesian Latin (el_pasifika):
  a,U+0061
  e,U+0065
  i,U+0069
  o,U+006F
  u,U+0075
  (uppercase variants omitted here for brevity)

You can also list consonants if your constraint is on specific bases:
  t,U+0074
  n,U+006E
  l,U+006C

Which diacritics can be applied? Format: one per line, comma-separated: diacritic_name,unicode_codepoint
Example for Polynesian Latin:
  macron,U+0304
  acute,U+0301
  diaeresis,U+0308
  grave,U+0300

These marks can be typed via deadkey, modifier-key, or sequence rule.
content.pattern.constraints_beep.title: Constraints + beep: reject invalid sequences with audio feedback
content.pattern.context_sensitive_substi: What a key types out depends on the character you typed just before it. Type a modifier character first, then press a trigger key — the result is determined by what you typed before, not just the key you pressed.

Which characters trigger a special output when followed by the trigger key? Type them without spaces. Each character here, when typed just before the trigger key, produces a different result than pressing the trigger key alone.
What does the trigger key produce for each context character above, in the same order? One output character per context, no spaces.
Internal name for this rule set (e.g. ipa_seq, tone_mod). Use letters and underscores only — it becomes a KMN identifier.
What does the trigger key produce when no special context character came immediately before it? This keeps the key working normally in all other cases.
Which key (pressed after the context character) produces a different output depending on what came before it? Example: K_EQUAL for the = key.
content.pattern.deadkey_single_tap.descr: Press an accent key (e.g. apostrophe), then a base letter — the accent key disappears and an accented character appears in its place. Pressing the accent key twice in a row produces the bare combining accent mark itself.
content.pattern.deadkey_single_tap.quest: The bare accent mark emitted when the trigger key is pressed twice (e.g. U+0301 for combining acute, U+0300 for combining grave).
The accented forms in the same order as the base letters above. Example: áéíóúÁÉÍÓÚ
Which base letters take this accent? Type them without spaces. Example: aeiouAEIOU
Internal name for this deadkey state (e.g. "acute", "grave", "circum"). Use letters and underscores only — it becomes a KMN identifier.
Which key starts the accent? (e.g. apostrophe, backtick, caret)
content.pattern.deadkey_single_tap.title: Tap-and-add accent
content.pattern.dll_ime_callout.descript: For logographic scripts with tens of thousands of characters (Han, Kanji, Hanja), the keyboard delegates character lookup to a native Windows IME via a DLL function call. Instead of embedding thousands of rules, the keyboard acts as a thin shim: user types a phonetic key sequence (Pinyin, Jyutping, Romaja), and the DLL (usually installed by the OS or IME vendor) returns the matching characters. Keyman rules simply call the DLL and pass through the result.
content.pattern.dll_ime_callout.question:
What DLL function will you call to look up characters? Format: DLL_NAME.DLL:FunctionName
Common examples:
  KeymanIMX.DLL:FindGlyph           (for Pinyin → Han, CS Pinyin keyboard)
  MSIMECallback.DLL:GetCharacters   (Microsoft IME callback)
  Custom.DLL:CustomLookup           (custom vendor DLL)

The DLL must be: - Installed on the user's Windows system (or shipped with the keyboard) - Accessible to Keyman at runtime - Callable with the signature the keyboard expects
Consult the IME vendor or DLL documentation for the exact function name and calling convention (what arguments it accepts, what it returns).

How does the user input the lookup key? (Pinyin, Jyutping, Romaja, etc.) Format: brief description + examples
Example for Simplified Chinese (Pinyin):
  - User types Pinyin romanization: "han" for 汉, "zi" for 字
  - The keyboard reads the keystroke sequence and passes it to the DLL
  - DLL returns matching Han characters; user selects from list

Example for Japanese (Romaji):
  - User types Romaji: "ka" for か, "sa" for さ
  - DLL returns kana or kanji matches
  - User selection appears in text

Describe your input scheme so future authors understand the flow.

Are you aware of the platform limitations? (Read carefully!)
S-12 is WINDOWS-ONLY. It does NOT work on: - macOS (no Windows.Media.Sounds equivalent for IME DLLs) - Linux (no native Windows DLL support) - Mobile (no Keyman DLL mechanism) - Web (no DLL access in browsers)
If you need cross-platform support, S-12 is NOT the right strategy. Consider: - S-05 (transliteration) if the inventory is <10k characters - Cloud-based lookup (not yet in Keyboard Studio v1) - Ship multiple platform-specific keyboards
Do you accept the Windows-only limitation and want to proceed with S-12?
content.pattern.dll_ime_callout.title: DLL IME callout: delegate to Windows IME for massive inventories
content.pattern.flick_gestures.descripti: Swipe up, down, left, or right on a key to produce a related character without opening a popup. Common on tone-marking and accent keyboards where direction maps naturally to meaning (e.g. swipe up for acute, down for grave).
Character produced by swiping DOWN on this key. Leave empty if no down-flick is needed.
Character produced by swiping LEFT on this key. Leave empty if no left-flick is needed.
Character produced by swiping RIGHT on this key. Leave empty if no right-flick is needed.
Character produced by swiping UP on this key. Leave empty if no up-flick is needed.
Which key carries the flick gestures? (virtual-key name, e.g. K_A)
content.pattern.flick_gestures.title: Swipe a key in a direction for a different letter
content.pattern.hint_characters.descript: Display a small hint character in the corner of a touch key so the user can see at a glance what a long-press or swipe will produce. The hint is a discoverability aid only — it does not change what the key types.
content.pattern.hint_characters.question: The small character or short string to show in the key corner. Usually the first longpress alternate or the primary flick output (e.g. "á"). Can also be a count string like "+ 3" to signal how many hidden characters exist. Keep it short — 1–3 characters is best.
Which key should display the hint? (virtual-key name, e.g. K_A)
content.pattern.hint_characters.title: Show a small letter in the corner of a key
content.pattern.indic_pre_base_vowel.des: Some vowels in Tamil (and several other Indic scripts) appear in front of the consonant on screen even though you type the consonant first, then the vowel. This is not a quirk of the keyboard — it is how the script works: the vowel sign belongs logically after the consonant in the stored text, and the rendering system places it visually to the left. This pattern stores the consonant and vowel in the correct Unicode order so the display comes out right without the keyboard author having to insert the vowel first or manipulate cursor position.
content.pattern.indic_pre_base_vowel.que: Enter the key names that produce each consonant (matching consonants order).
Enter the consonant characters this keyboard handles (one per slot, matching consonantKeys order).
What is the display name for this keyboard?
Enter the first pre-base vowel matra character (the one that displays to the left of the consonant)
Which key triggers the first pre-base vowel?
Enter the pre-base vowel matra characters (the ones that display before the consonant).
content.pattern.mnemonic_spelling.descri: User types an ASCII transliteration scheme (phonetically intuitive Latin spelling) and the keyboard expands it to the target character. Common for IPA, ITRANS (Sanskrit), romanized Greek, and any language where the user naturally thinks "I'd spell it this way." Multi-keystroke sequences build context-sensitive variants (e.g., "aa" → "ā" for length vowels, "RR" → "ṛ" for retroflex).
content.pattern.mnemonic_spelling.questi:
When you have overlapping sequences (e.g., "a" and "aa", "R" and "RR"), list the LONGER sequence first. The keyboard engine matches left-to-right and greedily (takes the first match), so order determines priority.
Example:
  WRONG order:  a,U+0905  /  aa,U+0906  → "aa" will never match
  RIGHT order:  aa,U+0906  /  a,U+0905  → "aa" matches first, then single "a"

Have you arranged your sequences by length (longest first)?

For each sequence, describe the output character in user-facing language. Format: ascii_sequence,description
Example for ITRANS:
  a,schwa (short a sound, like in 'about')
  aa,long a (like in 'father')
  R,retroflex r (r with tongue curled back)
  RR,retroflex long r

These descriptions appear in help docs and on-screen hints.

Define your transliteration scheme: ASCII sequences → target characters. Format: one entry per line, comma-separated: ascii_sequence,unicode_codepoint
**Single-keystroke mappings (base characters):**
  a,U+0905
  i,U+0907
  u,U+0909

**Multi-keystroke context-sensitive mappings (variants/length):**
  a a,U+0906        c second 'a' lengthens to ā
  i i,U+0908        c second 'i' lengthens to ī
  R R,U+0943        c 'RR' produces retroflex vowel ṛ

**Combining marks / modifiers (accents, tone marks):**
  m `,U+0902        c anusvara (nasal mark)
  n ~,U+0903        c visarga (aspiration mark)

Order matters: longer sequences should be listed BEFORE their prefixes. Example: list "RR" before "R" so "RR" is matched first.
Find Unicode codepoints at unicode.org or in the exemplar keyboard source.
content.pattern.mnemonic_spelling.title: Mnemonic spelling: type ASCII, get target character
content.pattern.modifier_as_layer_switch: Adds a second, hidden layer of characters that you reach by holding one extra key while pressing a normal key — like holding it to get currency symbols, punctuation, or rarely-used letters that don't fit on the main keyboard. This is the backbone of European-extended layouts: it is especially central to French-style (AZERTY) keyboards, where the held layer is how you reach €, #, and the { [ ] } brackets.

Which keys should gain a hidden-layer character, and in what order? For each one, pick the base key (such as E, 4, or 5) that you reach by holding the extra-layer key. The order you choose here must match the order of the characters below.
The hidden-layer characters, in the same order as the keys above — one character per key, typed without spaces. Example: é€£
Hold a key for a hidden layer
content.pattern.multi_char_sequence.desc: Each key you press shows its normal letter; when you type the second key, the pair already in the buffer is swapped for a single character (type n, then g, and the n becomes ŋ). The visible-output cousin of a deadkey. Use this only when your orthography treats the sequence as a single letter — if the same letters can also be two separate sounds, this would mis-collapse those words.
content.pattern.multi_char_sequence.ques: Which single character should the pair collapse to? (e.g. ŋ — U+014B, LATIN SMALL LETTER ENG)
What character does the FIRST key produce on its own? (e.g. n) This is matched as the already-typed context, not the key — so use the letter that appears on screen, not a key name.
What character does the SECOND key produce on its own? (e.g. g) Typing this letter after the first one fires the collapse.
content.pattern.multi_char_sequence.titl: Two letters in a row become one character
content.pattern.multitap.description: Tap the same key rapidly more than once to get a different letter each time. The first tap produces the key's main character; the second tap replaces it with the next character in the cycle; the third tap gives the next; and so on. This is the touch equivalent of T9-style cycling on feature phones and works well for syllabic scripts where a handful of related glyphs share one key.
content.pattern.multitap.question.hostKe: Which key should cycle on repeated taps? (virtual-key name, e.g. K_A)
content.pattern.multitap.question.multit: The characters to cycle through on the 2nd, 3rd, 4th … tap (in order). The first tap still produces the key's primary text; these entries take over on subsequent rapid taps. Type them without spaces, e.g. áàâ.
content.pattern.multitap.title: Tap a key several times to cycle through letters
content.pattern.nfd_latin.description: When a letter carries more than one mark — for example a vowel that is both nasalised and toned (such as an e with a mark below and an accent above) — the marks have to be stored in a precise sequence so that every program shows and searches the text correctly. This makes the keyboard put those marks in the right order behind the scenes, even when the author types them in a different order. You also choose whether the keyboard produces a single ready-made character when one exists, or always keeps the base letter and its marks as separate pieces — the safest choice when your language has combinations that have no ready-made form.
content.pattern.nfd_latin.question.combi: List the accent marks your language uses (the marks alone, not the finished accented letters). If you are not sure, leave this blank and the keyboard covers the common Latin marks automatically.
content.pattern.nfd_latin.question.outpu: Let the keyboard decide per character
Always keep base letter + marks as separate pieces
Single ready-made character where one exists
When the keyboard produces an accented letter that also exists as a single ready-made character, which form should it output?
content.pattern.nfd_latin.title: Stack accents in the right order
content.pattern.sea_stack_reorder.descri: Southeast Asian scripts (Khmer, Burmese, Lao) use subscript consonants to build consonant clusters. Subscripts are typed after the base consonant but must be stored before vowel signs so they render below the base. This pattern handles the reordering of subscripts, vowels, and tone marks to match the Unicode canonical order for syllables in these scripts. Thai does not use a coeng-style subscript marker and is excluded from this pattern.
content.pattern.sea_stack_reorder.questi:
Map each keystroke to the base consonant it produces, one per line. Format: keystroke,Unicode-codepoint Example for Khmer Angkor:
  K_K,U+1780
  K_G,U+1784
  K_D,U+178A

What is the Unicode codepoint for this script's subscript marker (without U+ prefix)? Example for Khmer: 17D2 (Khmer Sign Coeng, displays as ◌) Example for Burmese: 1039 (Myanmar Sign Virama, displays as ◌)

Map each keystroke to the subscript consonant it produces (after the coeng marker). Format: keystroke,Unicode-codepoint Example for Khmer Angkor:
  K_D,U+178F
  K_N,U+1793
  K_M,U+1798
List tone mark or diacritic codepoints that appear after vowels (space-separated, without U+ prefix). Leave blank if not applicable. Example for Khmer: 17CB 17CD 17CE 17CF 17D0 17D1
List all dependent vowel sign Unicode codepoints (space-separated, without U+ prefix). Example for Khmer: 17B6 17B7 17B8 17B9 17BA 17BB 17BC 17BD 17BE 17BF 17C0 17C1 17C2 17C3 17C4 17C5
content.pattern.sea_stack_reorder.title: Subscript consonants reorder: type after base, display below
content.pattern.simple_swap.description: Map 1–5 new characters onto unused keys. Each keystroke produces exactly one character. No deadkeys, no deadkey followup sequences, no diacritics, no case variants. Straight substitution.
content.pattern.simple_swap.question.key:
Map each keystroke to the character it produces. Format: one entry per line, comma-separated: keystroke,unicode_codepoint
Keystroke format:
  [K_X]           — unmodified key (where X is the virtual key name)
  [SHIFT K_X]     — with Shift
  [RALT K_X]      — with RAlt/AltGr
  [CTRL K_X]      — with Ctrl

**Finding virtual key names:** The K_ prefix maps to QWERTY physical positions:
  Top row: K_Q K_W K_E K_R K_T K_Y K_U K_I K_O K_P
  Home row: K_A K_S K_D K_F K_G K_H K_J K_K K_L
  Bottom row: K_Z K_X K_C K_V K_B K_N K_M
  Punctuation: K_COMMA K_PERIOD K_SLASH K_SEMICOLON K_QUOTE
  See Keyman docs at keyman.com for the complete virtual-key reference.

Example for Akan (adds ɛ and ɔ):
  [K_Q],U+025B
  [K_C],U+0254
  [SHIFT K_Q],U+0190
  [SHIFT K_C],U+0186

Find Unicode codepoints at unicode.org or copy from a reference keyboard.
content.pattern.simple_swap.question.swa:
For each character you're adding, provide a brief description for the user. Format: character,description
Example:
  ɛ,Latin small letter open e (like the 'e' in 'met')
  ɔ,Latin small letter open o (like the 'o' in 'dog')

These descriptions appear in the help docs and on-screen hints.
content.pattern.simple_swap.title: Simple swap: keystroke → single character
content.pattern.tone_mark_canonicalizati: Southeast Asian scripts (Thai, Lao) use tone marks that must appear in a fixed position relative to the syllable, regardless of typing order. The user can type the tone mark before, after, or in the middle of the syllable — the keyboard always stores it in the same canonical position (above the base consonant or vowel). This pattern uses context rules to reorder tone marks so the rendered output always has the correct visual appearance.

Which character classes can tone marks attach to? List the character classes with their Unicode codepoints. Format: class_name followed by codepoints (space-separated)
Example for Lao (from lao_2008_basic.kmn):
  Consonants: U+0E81 U+0E82 U+0E84 (etc.)
  Vowels: U+0EB4 U+0EB5 U+0EB6 U+0EB7 U+0EB8 U+0EB9 U+0EBB U+0EB1 U+0EB3

Tone marks typically attach to: - Vowels that appear above the consonant - Consonants (in syllables with no written vowel) - The last vowel in a sequence (for diphthongs)
List the characters that can precede a tone mark in canonical position.

What is the canonical position for tone marks in your script?
In Lao and Thai, the canonical order for a syllable is:
  (initial consonant) + (vowel signs) + (tone mark) + (final consonant)

Typing order varies by user and keyboard layout. Describe which reordering cases apply to your keyboard:
Case A: User types tone mark BEFORE the vowel
  typed: consonant + tone + vowel → store: consonant + vowel + tone

Case B: User types tone mark AFTER a vowel-already-in-place
  typed: consonant + vowel + tone → store: no reordering needed (already canonical)

Case C: Certain vowel sequences (like sala-am U+0EB3 in Lao) are typed
  AFTER the tone mark is already applied to the consonant:
  typed: consonant + tone + sala-am key → store: consonant + sala-am + tone
  (lao_2008_basic line 72 handles this)

List the cases relevant to your script.

Are there invalid combinations to block with a beep?
Example from lao_2008_basic.kmn (lines 76–88):
  any(Tone) + any(ToneKey) → beep       (no double tone marks)
  any(VPrefix) + any(ToneKey) → beep    (pre-vowel cannot take tone)
  any(VSuffix) + any(ToneKey) → beep    (post-vowel cannot take tone)
  any(Vowel) + any(VScriptKey) → beep   (no double vowel scripts)

If you want invalid combinations to beep (A6=loud, secondary S-10), list them here. Leave blank if all combinations should be allowed silently.

List all tone marks used in your script. Format: one per line, comma-separated: tone_name,unicode_codepoint
Example for Thai:
  mai_ek,U+0E48        (tone 1 — low falling)
  mai_tho,U+0E49       (tone 2 — falling)
  mai_tri,U+0E4A       (tone 3 — high)
  mai_chattawa,U+0E4B  (tone 4 — rising)
  (the fifth Thai tone, mid/sà·màan, is the default and has no mark)

Example for Lao:
  mai_ek,U+0EC8        (tone 1)
  mai_tho,U+0EC9       (tone 2)
  mai_ti,U+0ECA        (tone 3)
  mai_catawa,U+0ECB    (tone 4)
  (U+0ECC mai_kaan is a cancellation mark, distinct from tone marks
  and not part of a tone store)

Find codepoints at unicode.org or in the exemplar keyboard.
Tone mark canonicalization: reorder to canonical position
content.pattern.touch_layer_switch.descr: A dedicated key that swaps the entire keyboard to a different layer — numbers, symbols, punctuation, or an alternate script — so language experts can offer distinct character sets without crowding the main layout.
content.pattern.touch_layer_switch.quest: What is the ID of your main (opening) layer? This is the layer users see when the keyboard first appears. Use letters and hyphens only (e.g. "default", "main", "letters").
What text should appear on the key that returns from the secondary layer to the main layer? (e.g. "ABC", "abc", "Letters").
What text should appear on the key that switches from the main layer to the secondary layer? This is what the user sees and taps (e.g. "123", "?123", "!@#").
What is the ID of the secondary layer the switch key leads to? Common choices: "numbers", "symbols", "alt-script".
content.pattern.touch_layer_switch.title: Touch Layer Switch
